name: St. John's
intro: Colourful Atlantic city for cliffs, music and easternmost trips

history.label: 16th century
history.text: John's by Portuguese from the Azores took place in the early 16th century, and by 1540, French, Spanish and Portuguese ships crossed the Atlantic annually to fish the waters off the Avalon Peninsula.
When 1500 English reinforcements arrived in late 1697, they found rubble where the town and fortifications had stood.
The earliest record of the location appears as São João on a Portuguese map by Pedro Reinel in 1519.
John's in 1527, he found Norman, Breton and Portuguese ships in the harbour.
On 5 August 1583, an English Sea Dog, Sir Humphrey Gilbert, claimed the area as England's first overseas colony under Royal Charter of Queen Elizabeth I.
With respect to the oldest surviving permanent English settlements in North America, it was preceded by Jamestown, Virginia (1607), the Cuper's Cove colony at Cupids in Newfoundland (1610), St.

The English government planned to expand these fortifications (Fort William) in around 1689, but construction did not begin until after the French admiral Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville captured and destroyed the town in the.

history.label: 1700s
history.text: In the late 1700s, Fort Amherst and Fort Waldegrave were built to defend the harbour entrance. The oldest European settlement in Anglophone North America controversy There has been some controversy regarding which European settlement.
John's was destroyed by major fires in 1816, 1817, 1819, 1846 and 1892, when each time a large part of the city was destroyed.
There were two citywide fires in 1817 "known jointly as 'The Great Fire of 1817'".
The Knights of Columbus Hostel fire in December 1942 saw 99 military and civilian lives lost.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: John's International Airport substituting for Lester's Field (now an urban and residential part of the city).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: During the Second World War, the harbour supported Royal Navy and Royal Canadian Navy ships that were engaged in anti-submarine warfare.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: It was the site of an American Army Air Force base, Fort Pepperrell, that was established as part of the "Lend-Lease" Destroyers for Bases Agreement between the United Kingdom and United States.
